author: John Chrysostom
work: Homilies on the Gospel of Matthew
translation: NPNF ser. 1, vol. 10 (Oxford trans. by George Prevost, rev. M. B. Riddle), public domain
note: Chrysostom's classic call for ordinary lay Christians — not only monks — to read the Bible, from his Antiochene Homilies on Matthew (c. AD 390). Directly follows with the 'treasure' image (New Advent §11): 'Let us not therefore despise the hearing of the divine Scriptures. For this is of Satan's devising; not suffering us to see the treasure, lest we should gain the riches.' NOTE for the citations gate: the well-known line 'the reading of the Scriptures is a great safeguard against sin' is from Chrysostom's Homilies on Lazarus (De Lazaro, Concio 3), which is NOT in the NPNF corpus, so it is deliberately not quoted here; this Matthew II passage carries the same theme from a genuinely public-domain NPNF source. Wording follows New Advent's lightly modernized NPNF text (American spelling; 'you' for 'thou'); confirm verbatim vs source_url and, if the older 'thou' recension is preferred, against CCEL npnf110.iii.v.html (CORRECTED 2026-07-20: an earlier draft pointed at npnf110.iii.II.html, which serves the Oxford-edition preface, not Homily II; for the record the printed CCEL reads 'this is what hath ruined all' and 'when ye need it much more'). Verified verbatim against its cited source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20).
text: I am not, you will say, one of the monks, but I have both a wife and children, and the care of a household. Why, this is what has ruined all, your supposing that the reading of the divine Scriptures appertains to those only, when you need it much more than they. For they that dwell in the world, and each day receive wounds, these have most need of medicines.

author: John Chrysostom
work: Homilies on the Gospel of St. John
translation: NPNF ser. 1, vol. 14 (Oxford trans., rev. Philip Schaff), public domain
note: God's condescension (synkatabasis) in the incarnation — the Word 'in nothing diminished His own Nature' by stooping to us. A safe NPNF anchor for the synkatabasis theme: Chrysostom's most extended synkatabasis language is in his Homilies on Genesis, but those survive in English only in the modern (copyrighted) Fathers of the Church translation, NOT in the public-domain NPNF, so this Homilies on John passage is used instead. The ellipsis marks omitted words between the two quoted sentences; do NOT present as one continuous sentence. Confirm verbatim and the exact adjacency/section vs source_url (and CCEL npnf114) in the citations gate. verified:false. DELICACY: 'the Master took on Him the form of a servant' reads Arian/subordinationist if lifted alone. This quote is SELF-FENCING as stored - it carries 'who was God's own Son' and 'He in nothing diminished His own Nature by this condescension' - so it must never be trimmed to the first sentence. The ellipsis marks one omitted sentence; the two segments are in printed order (verified). Verified verbatim against its cited source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20).
text: It is that the Word became Flesh, that the Master took on Him the form of a servant. For He became Son of man, who was God's own Son, in order that He might make the sons of men to be children of God. … He in nothing diminished His own Nature by this condescension, but raised us, who had always sat in disgrace and darkness, to glory unspeakable.

author: John Chrysostom
work: Homilies on the Gospel of Matthew
translation: NPNF ser. 1, vol. 10 (Oxford trans. by George Prevost, rev. M. B. Riddle), public domain
note: A hallmark Chrysostom passage — 'the sacrament of the brother': the same Lord who said 'This is my body' also said 'you did it not to me.' Honouring Christ at the altar in gold while neglecting Him hungry in the poor is condemned. Devotional and widely loved; not intra-Christian-contested. Wording follows New Advent's lightly modernized NPNF ('Would you do honor', 'You saw me an hungered'); CORRECTED 2026-07-20: an earlier draft of this note claimed CCEL reads 'Wilt thou do honour... Ye saw me an hungred' - it does not; CCEL reads 'Wouldest thou do honor to Christ's body?' and 'Ye saw me an hungered' (American 'honor', and 'hungered'). Also corrected: the stored text is NOT stitched fragments - it is one continuous run with nothing omitted, apart from New Advent's inline scripture locator '[Matthew 25:42, 45]', which is an editorial marker rather than translated text. Verified verbatim against its cited source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20).
text: Would you do honor to Christ's body? Neglect Him not when naked; do not while here you honor Him with silken garments, neglect Him perishing without of cold and nakedness. For He that said, This is my body, and by His word confirmed the fact, This same said, You saw me an hungered, and fed me not; and, Inasmuch as you did it not to one of the least of these, you did it not to me. For This indeed needs not coverings, but a pure soul; but that requires much attention.

author: John Chrysostom
work: Homilies on the Epistle to the Philippians
translation: NPNF ser. 1, vol. 13 (Oxford trans.), public domain
note: Chrysostom on Christ's self-emptying (Phil. 2) — He who was in the form of God took the form of a servant. Preached during the Constantinople period (c. AD 398–404). Note this NPNF exposition, like the whole homily, guards the deity: Chrysostom insists the kenosis is an assumption of humanity, not a diminishing of the divine nature (the homily also carries the anti-kenotic formula 'while He remained what He was, He took that which He was not, and being made flesh He remained God' — capture/verify separately if wanted). Confirm the quoted sentences verbatim and the exact section vs source_url in the citations gate. verified:false. DELICACY (the highest-risk entry in this file, now fenced STRUCTURALLY rather than by advice): the bare 'He emptied Himself... form of a servant' is indistinguishable from kenoticism - the error that Christ laid aside His deity - if quoted alone. The stored text has therefore been EXTENDED (the ellipsis marks the omitted span) to carry Chrysostom's own anti-kenotic conclusion from the same homily: 'while He remained what He was, He took that which He was not, and being made flesh He remained God.' Never quote the first half without the second. Section corrected 2026-07-20: the printed heading is Phil. ii. 5-11, not 5-8. Verified verbatim against its cited source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20).
text: He emptied Himself. How did He empty Himself? By taking the form of a servant, being made in the likeness of men, and being found in fashion as a man. … For lest when you hear that He emptied Himself, you should think that some change, and degeneracy, and loss is here; he says, while He remained what He was, He took that which He was not, and being made flesh He remained God, in that He was the Word.

author: John Chrysostom
work: Homilies on the First Epistle to the Corinthians
translation: NPNF ser. 1, vol. 12 (Oxford trans., rev. Talbot W. Chambers), public domain
note: DENOMINATIONAL-NEUTRALITY NOTE (required): cite this ONLY for the antiquity and depth of eucharistic reverence in the early church — NOT to settle the Catholic/Orthodox/Protestant debate over the mode of Christ's presence. Chrysostom's language is strongly realist, but Apologia Daily does not adjudicate that second-order intra-Christian dispute; use it for the common ground (awe and self-examination in approaching the Table, echoing 1 Cor 11:28). Quote truncated at 'but free' (the sentence continues in the source); wording follows New Advent's NPNF text. Confirm verbatim vs source_url in the citations gate. verified:false. [HELD AT verified:false, 2026-07-20 - NOT quotable.] The citations gate found the stored text is a HYBRID of two recensions: 'say thou to thyself' is the printed CCEL/NPNF reading grafted onto an otherwise New Advent sentence (New Advent reads 'say thou to yourself'). It is also truncated mid-sentence at 'but free' with no ellipsis; the sentence continues '...: because of this I hope for heaven, and to receive the good things therein, immortal life, the portion of angels, converse with Christ.' TO RESOLVE: re-base the whole quote on ONE host - preferably the printed CCEL text at https://www.ccel.org/ccel/schaff/npnf112.iv.xxv.html with its archaic forms ('draw nigh', 'when thou seest', 'say thou to thyself') - and extend it to 'converse with Christ.' Section header corrected: the homily is on 1 Cor. x. 13, not x. 16-17.
text: Let us not, I pray you, let us not slay ourselves by our irreverence, but with all awfulness and purity draw near to It; and when you see It set before you, say thou to thyself, Because of this Body am I no longer earth and ashes, no longer a prisoner, but free.

author: John Chrysostom
work: Homilies on the Epistle to the Ephesians
translation: NPNF ser. 1, vol. 13 (tr. Gross Alexander), public domain
note: Chrysostom on salvation as God's free grace, echoing Eph. 2:8–9. DENOMINATIONAL-NEUTRALITY NOTE: this passage borders the Catholic–Protestant justification debate; use it for the shared, creedal affirmation that salvation is God's unmerited gift (grace over human boasting), NOT as a proof text for a particular doctrine of justification — Chrysostom elsewhere strongly affirms the necessity of works/human cooperation (synergy), and in this same homily says 'how does faith save, without works? This itself is the gift of God.' Present the whole, do not weaponize one half. Confirm verbatim and exact section vs source_url in the citations gate. verified:false. DELICACY (denominational neutrality, now fenced STRUCTURALLY): 'saved us by grace; so that no man may have whereof to boast' is weaponizable in the justification dispute if quoted bare. The stored text has therefore been EXTENDED BACKWARD to open with Chrysostom's own guard - 'Hath He Himself hindered our being justified by works? By no means.' - so the fence travels with the quote instead of living only in this note. Cite for the antiquity of grace-language held in common; do NOT enlist it to settle the Catholic/Protestant justification debate. Section corrected 2026-07-20: the printed heading is Eph. ii. 1-3 (the exposition runs on to ii. 10, where the quoted lines sit), not ii. 8-10. Verified verbatim against its cited source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20).
text: What then? says a man, Hath He Himself hindered our being justified by works? By no means. But no one, he says, is justified by works, in order that the grace and loving-kindness of God may be shown. He did not reject us as having works, but as abandoned of works He has saved us by grace; so that no man henceforth may have whereof to boast.
